title: What does a per-user salt prevent?
prompt_md: When hashing passwords, you mix a unique random **salt** into each password before hashing. What does the salt protect against?
options: It encrypts the password so it can be decrypted later by the server
It makes the hash function run faster so logins are quicker
It defeats precomputed rainbow tables and stops identical passwords from producing identical hashes
It hides the username from anyone reading the database
explanation_md: A **salt** is a unique random value added to each password before hashing. Two benefits: (1) attackers can't use **precomputed rainbow tables** — giant lookup tables of common-password→hash — because those tables didn't include your random salt, so they'd have to rebuild them per salt; and (2) two users who happen to pick the same password get **different hashes**, so an attacker can't spot reused or shared passwords by scanning for identical hash values. A salt does not encrypt anything (hashing is one-way, not reversible), doesn't speed hashing up, and has nothing to do with hiding usernames.
